1: “In the spring, at the time when kings went to war” (lĕtĕšûḇat haššānâ, lit. “at the return of the year”) — a conventional ANE formula marking the spring campaign season. After winter rains ended (roughly April-May), roads became passable for armies and logistics, and the harvest was approaching. This exact phrase (in 2 Samuel 11:1) introduces the Bathsheba-Uriah narrative, which the Chronicler notably omits. By jumping directly to the capture of Rabbah, the Chronicler silently elides David’s greatest moral failure while preserving the military accomplishment.
2: The Ammonite king’s crown weighed a talent of gold (about 75 lbs/34 kg) — too heavy for a human to actually wear, suggesting either a symbolic/votive crown or a crown placed on a divine statue. The Hebrew malkām can be translated either “their king” (Hanun) or as the proper name Milcom/Molech, the Ammonite national deity (1 Kings 11:5). The LXX reads “Milcom,” and many scholars follow this: David takes the crown from the Ammonite god’s statue as a trophy, symbolizing YHWH’s victory over the competing deity.
3: David’s treatment of the Rabbah inhabitants — put to work with saws, iron picks, and axes — describes forced labor rather than execution (the Hebrew can be read either way; 1 Chronicles 20:3 uses wayyāśar = he sawed; 2 Samuel 12:31 also mentions similar treatment). The use of conquered populations for building projects was standard ANE practice: the Assyrians and Babylonians routinely employed captured peoples in state construction. David’s action places the Ammonite population in the corvée system — labor obligation to the state.
4: The Philistine wars at Gezer — Sibbecai the Hushathite killing Sippai (= Saph in 2 Samuel 21:18). Gezer was a strategically crucial city on the western approach to Jerusalem, controlling the valley road from the coast. It remained semi-independent through much of the monarchy; Solomon received it from Pharaoh as a dowry (1 Kings 9:16). These Philistine giant-slaying episodes were likely originally part of a hero-list tradition celebrating David’s warriors.
5: The textual variant: 1 Chronicles 20:5 attributes the kill to “Elhanan son of Jair” who killed “Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite,” while 2 Samuel 21:19 reads “Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim” who killed “Goliath the Gittite” (without “the brother of”). Chronicles appears to harmonize the apparent conflict with 1 Sam 17 (where David kills Goliath) by adding “the brother of.” This is likely a scribal correction in the Chronicler’s source; the earlier tradition in Samuel preserves the more difficult reading.
6: The giant with six fingers and six toes (twelve digits per hand/foot) at Gath — polydactyly, a rare congenital condition. The association of this anomaly with the Rephaim (pre-Israelite giant peoples of Canaan, Deuteronomy 2:10-12; 1 Chronicles 3:11; Genesis 14:5) may reflect a tradition of unusual physical traits among this group. Goliath’s spear “like a weaver’s rod” (1 Samuel 17:7) and his height (6 cubits and a span = approximately 9 feet, or 4.5 cubits = approximately 6.5 feet in some manuscripts) are part of this warrior-giant tradition.
7: Jonathan son of Shimea (David’s brother) kills the six-fingered giant. The naming of David’s nephew as the hero here fits a pattern: David’s inner family circle participates in the giant-slaying tradition, which the narrator connects with the broader elimination of the Rephaim threat to Israel. The Rephaim as supernatural or semi-divine beings appear in Ugaritic texts (rpum) as a class of deified warrior-heroes; their ANE background gives the Israelite tradition a demythologizing edge.
8: The summary formula — “these were the descendants of the Rephaim in Gath, and they fell at the hands of David and his warriors” — closes the Philistine war episodes. The Chronicler uses this unit to demonstrate the completeness of David’s military achievement: all major enemies (Moab, Aram, Edom, Ammon, Philistia) have been subdued. The rest David achieves through warfare becomes the pre-condition for Solomon’s temple-building era of peace.
